weatherForecast.comment: Nocturnal snowfall will taper off during the course of the day on Friday. Northerly winds will bring cold mountain weather and generally poor visibility. Temperature at 2000m: from -6 degrees; at 3000m: -11 degrees. Brisk to strong-velocity northerly winds.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Winter sports enthusiasts beware: still high avalanche danger widespread
avalancheActivity.comment: The large amounts of fresh snow and drifts are prone to triggering. Avalanches can easily be triggered by one single winter sports enthusiast in all aspects. In isolated cases, also naturally triggered avalanches can be expected. Exposed areas can be placed at risk. Danger zones occur on steep slopes, behind drops in the landscape and in wind-loaded gullies and bowls. Frequency and spread of avalanche prone locations increase with ascending altitude. Whumpf noises and fractures which appear when treading upon the snowpack, as well as freshly released avalanches are alarm signals. Activities in outlying terrain are currently dangerous.

snowpackStructure.comment: By Friday midday additional 10-20cm of fresh snow, locally more, is anticipated. The large amounts of fresh fallen snow have continually been transported in different directions, new snowdrift accumulations generated, but they are difficult to recognize since constantly getting blanketed with fresh snowfall. Bonding to the unfavorable snowpack is usually weak. Several large and very large avalanche releases from artificial (explosive) triggerings, remote triggerings and triggerings by winter sports enthusiasts confirm just how prone to triggering the snowpack is.

tendency.highlights: Frequency of naturally triggered avalanches is receding at high altitudes due to decreased precipitation.  For winter sports enthusiasts the situation remains treacherous. As a result of the forecast higher temperatures, wet-snow and glide-snow avalanches will be possible particularly at low and intermediate altitudes.

weatherForecast.comment: Nocturnal snowfall will taper off during the course of the day on Friday. Northerly winds will bring cold mountain weather and generally poor visibility. Temperature at 2000m: from -6 degrees; at 3000m: -11 degrees. Brisk to strong-velocity northerly winds.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Considerable avalanche danger at high altitudes.
avalancheActivity.comment: Considerable avalanche danger prevails. Avalanches can easily be triggered by one single winter sports enthusiast. Danger zones occur on steep slopes in all aspects, behind drops in the landscape and in wind-loaded gullies and bowls. Frequency and spread of avalanche prone locations increase with ascending altitude. Whumpf noises and fractures which appear when treading upon the snowpack, as well as freshly released avalanches are alarm signals. Activities in outlying terrain demand cautious assessment of the terrain and careful evaluation of the dangers. The inexperienced should not leave secured ski runs.

snowpackStructure.comment: By Friday midday additional 10-20cm of fresh snow, locally more, is anticipated. The large amounts of fresh fallen snow have continually been transported in different directions, new snowdrift accumulations generated, but they are difficult to recognize since constantly getting blanketed with fresh snowfall. Fresh snow/drifts have settled slightly, but bonding to the unfavorable snowpack is usually weak.

tendency.highlights: Avalanche danger is diminishing only gradually. As a result of the forecast higher temperatures, wet-snow and glide-snow avalanches will be possible.

ALBINA.generalHeadline: Ongoingly high avalanche danger in some regions
